Start with: “My feet and ankles feel weak, and I keep tripping over nothing.” Only add if asked or prompted: stumbling on curbs/uneven ground, toes not lifting when tired, high arches worsening and shoes not fitting, calves getting skinnier.

Answer questions briefly (1–2 sentences). If asked directly, admit tingling/numb toes, easy ankle rolls/sprains, slower stairs/can’t run, subtle hand weakness (jars/buttons), not noticing small cuts/blisters, and that it’s been slowly worsening over years.

Do NOT volunteer: high-stepping gait, shoe wear on front/outside, curling toes/sore spots, avoiding dark, worse with stress/sickness, worse balance with eyes closed—only reveal if specifically asked about gait, balance, shoes, or falls.

Downplay falls/embarrassment unless asked; resist discussing dating/anxiety/workouts.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s a pinched nerve/poor circulation/MS/diabetes.” Be skeptical about PT (“too expensive, want a pill”), fear job accommodations/firing, and assume genetic testing is for rich people and could raise insurance. If clinician shows empathy, respond with a short, guarded “Yeah… just want to keep working.”

comorbidities: Neuropathic pain related to Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
Chronic ankle instability with recurrent sprains
Mild vitamin B12 deficiency (functional; elevated methylmalonic acid)
confounders: Functional vitamin B12 deficiency can contribute to distal numbness/paresthesias and mimic/worsen neuropathy
History of heavy alcohol use in early 20s (stopped 1 year ago) can cause peripheral neuropathy and complicate attribution of symptoms
Intermittent use of high-dose naproxen for ankle pain may mask inflammatory symptoms and delay evaluation of new neurologic changes
Family history is unclear due to adoption, making hereditary neuropathy less obvious on initial assessment

required_evidence: Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern (distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss)
Pes cavus or foot deformities and frequent ankle sprains/foot drop
Slowly progressive course beginning in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood
Exclude common acquired causes with available labs (normal A1c/fasting glucose, B12/MMA, TSH, CK, ESR/CRP)

Gabapentin 300 mg PO qHS, increase to 300 mg PO TID as tolerated for neuropathic pain
non_pharmacological: Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Genetic counseling and genetic testing referral
Avoid neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine)
